sections.heading: Overview
sections.paragraph: The pharmaceutical landscape for targeted protein degradation is rapidly evolving, with Proteolysis Targeting Chimera (PROTAC) technology solidifying its clinical viability following the U.S. Food and Drug Administration approval of Arvinas Inc.’s VEPPANU (vepdegestrant) for locally advanced or metastatic ER+/HER2- breast cancer. This milestone validates the PROteolysis TArgeting Chimera platform, which selectively degrades disease-causing proteins rather than merely inhibiting them. Concurrently, the proximity medicine sector is expanding beyond traditional PROTACs, with Vienna-based Proxygen securing a $2.55 billion partnership with Merck to develop next-generation molecular glue degraders targeting p300 and CDK12. While molecular glues offer advantages in blood-brain barrier penetration, PROTACs remain pivotal for targeting undruggable proteins, evidenced by Arvinas’ ongoing programs for KRAS G12D and LRRK2. Innovation continues with Johnson & Johnson-owned Halda Therapeutics pioneering Regulated Induced Proximity Targeted Chimeras (RIPTACs) via HLD-0915, employing a “hold and kill” mechanism independent of the proteasome. Significant capital flows into the sector are further evidenced by AbbVie’s $1.64 billion agreement with Neomorph and Takeda’s $1.2 billion licensing deal with Degron Therapeutics, signaling robust industry confidence in proximity-based therapeutics for oncology and neurological conditions. Fortitude Biomedicines is also advancing Glue DACs to overcome antibody-drug conjugate resistance.

points.text: Arvinas — vepdegestrant (VEPPANU) — ER+/HER2- Breast Cancer — FDA Approval
Proxygen — Molecular Glue Degraders — Multiple Targets — Merck Partnership
Halda Therapeutics — HLD-0915 — Cancer — Clinical Entry
Fortitude Biomedicines — Glue DAC — ADC Resistance — Discovery Stage
AbbVie — Neomorph — Immunology/Cancer — $1.64 Billion Deal
Takeda — Degron Therapeutics — Oncology/Neuroscience — $1.2 Billion Deal
Arvinas: Clinical-stage biotech pioneering PROTAC platform with multiple candidates in development.
Proxygen: Vienna-based biotech developing next-generation molecular glue degraders with covalent pharmacology.
Halda Therapeutics: Subsidiary of Johnson & Johnson pioneering RIPTAC technology for cancer.
Fortitude Biomedicines: Developing Glue DACs to expand therapeutic window of antibody-drug conjugates.
vepdegestrant: PROTAC mechanism targeting estrogen receptor, approved for ESR1m, ER+/HER2- breast cancer.
ARV-806: Degrades KRAS G12D, targeting pancreatic, colorectal, and non-small cell lung cancers.
HLD-0915: RIPTAC mechanism binds target and pan-essential protein, oral small molecule, early results encouraging.
Proxygen p300: Covalent molecular glue, suppresses oncogenic transcriptional programs, preclinical anti-tumor activity.
Proxygen CDK12: Eliminates cyclin K; disrupts CDK12-dependent transcriptional programs; high brain penetrance.
Molecular Glues: Bind E3 ubiquitin ligase to create ternary complex; smaller than PROTACs; effective in crossing blood-brain barrier.
Arvinas and Pfizer collaboration on vepdegestrant (Veppanu) development and commercialization.
Merck and Proxygen $2.55 billion agreement to identify and develop molecular glue degraders.
Johnson & Johnson acquisition of Halda Therapeutics to advance RIPTAC pipeline.
AbbVie — Neomorph: $1.64 billion deal to create glues for immunology and cancer targets.
Takeda — Degron Therapeutics: $1.2 billion licensing deal to advance degraders for oncology, neuroscience, and inflammation.
